title: De-escalating the Agitated Patient
subtitle: The nurse calms an escalating patient — keeping everyone safe, using a calm voice and personal space, setting clear limits, and saving restraints as a last resort.

patient.name: Agitation / De-escalation
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely de-escalates an agitated patient.

nursesNote: The nurse safely de-escalates an agitated patient, keeping the safety of the patient, staff, and others as the top priority. The nurse watches for early warning signs of escalation, such as pacing, a loud or rising voice, clenched fists, and a tense posture, and intervenes early. The approach matters: the nurse maintains a calm, non-threatening stance with hands visible, respects the patient's personal space, keeps a clear path to the exit, and does not corner the patient or turn their back. A low, slow voice with short and simple words helps, and the nurse reduces stimulation by lowering noise and removing an audience. The nurse listens, acknowledges the patient's concern, and offers reasonable choices to restore a sense of control, while setting clear, firm limits without threats or arguing. Help is called early, and the nurse does not face an escalating patient alone. A PRN medication may be offered or given if ordered. Restraints and seclusion are a last resort, used only when less restrictive measures fail and the patient is a danger; they require a provider order, the least restrictive type, and close, frequent monitoring. The nurse documents the behaviors, interventions, and the patient's response.
providerOrders: Ensure the safety of the patient, staff, and others
Recognize early signs of escalation and intervene early
Use a calm voice, respect personal space, and keep an exit clear
Set clear limits and offer choices to restore control
Use restraints only as a last resort with an order and monitoring

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about managing agitation?

opts.text: Safety of everyone is the top priority
Pacing and clenched fists are early warning signs
A calm, low voice helps de-escalate
Cornering the patient is a good control method
Respecting personal space lowers the threat
Restraints are a routine first response
Keeping a clear path to the exit is important
Offering choices can restore a sense of control
Arguing and threatening calm the patient
The nurse should call for help early
Reducing noise and an audience helps
Clear, firm limits should still be set

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order responding to a patient who is becoming agitated

opts.text: Ensure your own safety and a clear exit
Call for help/backup
Use a calm voice and respect personal space
Listen, acknowledge, and offer choices
Offer a PRN medication if needed and ordered
Speaking calmly with short, simple words
Standing close and pointing a finger
Offering the patient reasonable choices
Arguing and raising your voice back
Reducing noise and removing the audience
Blocking the only exit from the room

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient is shouting, throwing items, and threatening staff
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Protect yourself and others; create distance
Call for help and clear the area
Try verbal de-escalation with a calm voice
Give a PRN medication if ordered
Use restraints as a last resort with an order
Intervening at the first signs of agitation
Turning your back on an escalating patient
Keeping a calm posture with hands visible
Trying to handle a violent patient alone
Using the least restrictive measure first
Applying restraints without a provider order
Monitoring a restrained patient frequently
Threatening punishment to force cooperation
